_description: Persistent record of witnessed events at a location. NPCs who witnessed an event can remember, forget, misremember, or retell it. This is what makes the world remember (Article XXXI.5). The existing WorldHistory system writes to this ledger; KnowledgeEngine lets NPCs retell entries they witnessed. This is DATA, not a Java class (Article XXVI).
_canonical_test: Week 1 — Old Chen owns a dog. Week 3 — wolf attack — dog dies. Week 5 — Old Chen no longer mentions the dog; says 'Used to have one.' Months later — a child tells the story. When this sequence occurs WITHOUT developer scripting the specific beat, the world has memory. This ledger is the data structure that makes it possible.

_design_notes.decay_model: Every memory has a memory_decay block. 'linear' reduces recall probability by a fixed amount per tick. 'exponential' halves recall every half_life_ticks. 'none' means the memory is permanent (canonical events). When recall probability drops below forget_threshold, the NPC no longer mentions it — but it is not deleted; a trigger (a question, a similar event) can resurrect it.
_design_notes.distortion: Each retelling may distort the memory. The distortion_over_time field describes how. A dog becomes 'a hound.' A wolf becomes 'a demon wolf.' A death becomes 'a sacrifice.' This is how rumors and legends form — and how the player, arriving late, hears a changed version of the truth.
_design_notes.witnesses_vs_participants: Witnesses perceived the event (saw, heard, smelled). Participants were directly involved. A witness can retell; a participant can retell with more detail. A non-witness can only retell if they heard it from a witness — and then it is a rumor, not a memory.
_design_notes.retelling_hooks: Each event has a retelling block listing who can retell it and lines they might use. KnowledgeEngine selects a line based on the reteller's relationship to the event, the time since, and the distortion level. A child retelling months later uses the most distorted line.
_design_notes.consequences: Memory entries can carry consequences — world-state changes that persist. The dog's death might reduce Old Chen's morale permanently, shift his daily_schedule (no more walks to the south bend), and seed a rumor that the south bend is dangerous.

schema.location: string — the location this ledger tracks

events.event_id: string — unique within the ledger, e.g. 'dog_died_wolf_attack_001'
events.event_type: enum — birth | death | raid | cultivation_breakthrough | disappearance | arrival | departure | betrayal | gift | lesson | discovery | loss | marriage | scandal | miracle | catastrophe | ordinary_day
events.timestamp_tick: int — the tick the event occurred (0 = pre-simulation / seed)
events.description: string — what happened, in plain language, as a participant would describe it
witnesses: string — npc_ids who perceived the event

memory_decay.half_life_ticks: int — for exponential; ticks for recall probability to halve
memory_decay.forget_threshold: float 0-1 — below this, the NPC no longer mentions it unprompted
memory_decay.permanent: bool — if true, never forgets (canonical events)

retell_lines: string — a line, used based on time since event and reteller
retelling.distortion_over_time: string — how the story changes. e.g. 'dog -> hound -> demon hound after 30 days; wolf -> spirit wolf -> demon wolf after 60 days'
trigger_topics: string — conversation topics that surface this memory, e.g. 'dog', 'south_bend', 'wolves', 'hunting'

consequences: string — world-state changes. e.g. 'old_chen.morale-=3 (permanent)', 'old_chen.schedule: no south_bend walks', 'south_bend.danger_reputation+=2', 'rumor_seed: south_bend_haunted'
tags: string — searchable tags for the engine

rumor_seeds.origin_event_id: string — the event this rumor distorted from
rumor_seeds.current_form: string — the current distorted version in circulation
believers: string — npc_ids who believe this version
rumor_seeds.decays_to: string — what the rumor becomes if unchecked, or 'fades' if it dies
